subject.name: Electrical Installation
subject.description: BS 7671, safe isolation, cables, protective devices and testing.

quizzes.title: Safe Isolation Procedure
quizzes.description: The sequence that keeps you alive. Get it in the right order.

questions.text: Put the safe isolation procedure into the correct sequence.
items: Identify the circuit to be isolated
Prove the voltage indicator on a known live source or proving unit
Switch off and isolate the circuit at the point of isolation
Verify the circuit is dead with the voltage indicator
Re-prove the voltage indicator on the known source
Lock off the isolator and retain the only key
Fit a caution notice at the point of isolation
questions.explain: Prove → isolate → test dead → re-prove. Re-proving after the test is the step people skip, and it is the one that catches a failed tester.

questions.text: Which device must be used to prove a voltage indicator before and after testing dead?
options: A proving unit or known live supply
questions.explain: GS38 rules out neon screwdrivers entirely. A proving unit gives a known live source you can trust.

questions.text: It is acceptable to leave a lock-off device fitted and give the key to the site supervisor while you work.
questions.explain: The person working on the circuit retains the only key. Handing it over means someone else can re-energise the circuit.

questions.text: Select ALL items of PPE/equipment required for safe isolation of a domestic consumer unit.
questions.explain: Ear defenders and hi-vis are not part of the isolation procedure — though site rules may require them anyway.

quizzes.title: BS 7671 Wiring Regulations
quizzes.description: Cable identification, RCD protection and zones.

questions.text: In this 13A plug top, which labelled conductor is the CPC (earth)?
questions.imageAlt: Cutaway of a UK 13A plug showing conductors labelled A to E
options: A — green/yellow to the top pin
B — blue to the left pin
questions.explain: Earth goes to the longest top pin, green/yellow, and is left slightly longer than L and N so it is last to disconnect if the cord is pulled.

questions.text: Click the fuse in the plug top.
questions.explain: The fuse sits in the line (brown) conductor only, so a blown fuse leaves the appliance dead but still connected to neutral.

questions.text: Under BS 7671, what is the maximum disconnection time for a 230 V final circuit up to 63 A on a TN system?
questions.explain: 0.4 s for final circuits up to 63 A on TN. 5 s applies to distribution circuits.

questions.text: Which colour is the line conductor in a modern harmonised twin & earth cable?
questions.explain: Brown = line, blue = neutral since harmonisation in 2004. Red/black is the pre-2004 system.

questions.text: Match each protective device to what it primarily protects against.

pairs.right: Both overcurrent and earth leakage on one circuit

questions.explain: An RCD does not protect against overload — that is why RCBOs exist.

questions.text: A 230 V circuit draws 9.2 A. What is the resistance of the load, in ohms? (Give to 1 decimal place.)

quizzes.title: Fault Finding Scenarios
quizzes.description: Think like a spark on a call-out.

questions.scenario: A customer reports that the RCD protecting the downstairs sockets trips every time the kettle is switched on, but never at any other time. Other circuits are unaffected.
questions.text: What is the most likely fault?
options: A deteriorated element in the kettle causing earth leakage under load
The RCD is undersized for the circuit
A loose neutral at the consumer unit
questions.explain: Leakage only appears when the element heats and draws current. Overload would trip the MCB, not the RCD.

questions.scenario: During an insulation resistance test on a lighting circuit you record 0.02 MΩ between line and earth. All lamps have been removed and the circuit is isolated.
options: Record it as a pass — it is above zero
Investigate: the reading is far below the 1 MΩ minimum, indicating a fault
Increase the test voltage to 1000 V and retest
questions.explain: BS 7671 requires a minimum of 1 MΩ for a 500 V test on a 230 V circuit. 0.02 MΩ means damaged insulation or moisture ingress.

questions.text: A low Zs reading is generally a good result on an earth fault loop impedance test.
questions.explain: Lower Zs means more fault current, so the protective device operates faster. It must be below the maximum tabulated value for the device.
